content.text: Azure Arc for System Center Virtual Machine Manager (SCVMM) projects Microsoft Hyper-V workloads managed by SCVMM into Azure Resource Manager. Like Arc for VMware, it uses an Arc Resource Bridge — a Kubernetes appliance VM deployed inside the SCVMM environment — to relay ARM control signals to SCVMM. Once connected, operators can view and manage Hyper-V VMs from the Azure portal, deploy new VMs from SCVMM VM templates, and enable Arc guest management on individual VMs to unlock extensions and governance features.

attrs.alt: Arc for SCVMM — Resource Bridge, SCVMM, Hyper-V hosts, Arc VMs, Azure ARM

content.text: Arc for SCVMM projects Microsoft.ScVmm/* resources, which are distinct from Microsoft.ConnectedVMwarevSphere/* (VMware) and Microsoft.AzureStackHCI/* (Azure Stack HCI). The RBAC roles, ARM resource types, and az scvmm CLI subcommand are all SCVMM-specific — do not mix CLI commands between the VMware and SCVMM codepaths.
Arc for SCVMM vs Arc for VMware vs Azure Stack HCI
Azure Hybrid Benefit from Azure portal
Azure Hybrid Benefit from Azure portal
Deploying the Arc Resource Bridge for SCVMM

The Arc Resource Bridge for SCVMM is a Hyper-V VM (not ESXi) managed by SCVMM itself. The az scvmm CLI extension and az arcappliance CLI extension together orchestrate the deployment. The process creates the appliance VM through SCVMM, then registers it in Azure.

# Install required CLI extensions
az extension add --name arcappliance
az extension add --name scvmm

# 1. Generate configuration for the Arc Resource Bridge
az arcappliance createconfig scvmm   --resource-group $RG   --name arc-bridge-scvmm   --location eastus   --out-dir ./arc-scvmm-config

# Wizard prompts for:
#   SCVMM server FQDN/IP and credentials (domain admin or SCVMM admin)
#   Host group (where the bridge VM will be placed)
#   VM network and IP pool (for appliance static IP)
#   VM template or ISO for the appliance base image

# 2. Deploy the appliance VM through SCVMM
az arcappliance deploy scvmm   --config-file ./arc-scvmm-config/arc-bridge-scvmm-appliance.yaml

# 3. Register the appliance in Azure ARM
az arcappliance create scvmm   --resource-group $RG   --name arc-bridge-scvmm   --location eastus   --kubeconfig ./arc-scvmm-config/arc-bridge-scvmm-kubeconfig

# 4. Verify appliance is Running
az arcappliance show -g $RG -n arc-bridge-scvmm   --query "{status:status, version:properties.version}"

# 5. Connect SCVMM Server to Azure
az scvmm vmmserver connect   --resource-group $RG   --name scvmm-prod   --location eastus   --custom-location arc-bridge-scvmm   --fqdn scvmm.contoso.local   --port 8100   --username svc-arc@contoso.com   --password "$SCVMM_PASSWORD"

# 6. Trigger inventory sync (VMs, templates, networks, storage appear in Azure)
az scvmm vmmserver show -g $RG -n scvmm-prod   --query "{connectionStatus:properties.connectionStatus, provisioningState:provisioningState}"

After inventory sync, Hyper-V VMs appear in the Azure portal under the SCVMM resource. Power operations and VM creation use the az scvmm vm CLI or ARM REST API — SCVMM handles the actual Hyper-V calls. VM templates and VM networks defined in SCVMM are projected as ARM resources.

# List SCVMM VM inventory synced to Azure
az scvmm vm list -g $RG   --query "[].{name:name, powerState:properties.powerState,                osType:properties.osProfile.osType,                host:properties.infrastructureProfile.hostRef.name}"

# Power operations (SCVMM executes the Hyper-V command)
az scvmm vm start -g $RG -n "vm-win-prod-01"
az scvmm vm stop  -g $RG -n "vm-win-prod-01"
az scvmm vm restart -g $RG -n "vm-win-prod-01"

# List available VM templates from SCVMM inventory
az scvmm vm-template list -g $RG   --query "[].{name:name, osType:properties.osType, cpus:properties.cpuCount, memMB:properties.memoryMB}"

# Create a new VM from a SCVMM template
az scvmm vm create   --resource-group $RG   --name "vm-linux-new-01"   --location eastus   --custom-location arc-bridge-scvmm   --vmmserver-name scvmm-prod   --vm-template-name "Ubuntu-2204-LTS"   --cloud-name "Production-Cloud"   --cpu-count 4   --memory-size 8192   --dynamic-memory-enabled true

# Resize an existing VM (requires it to be stopped first in most SCVMM versions)
az scvmm vm stop -g $RG -n "vm-linux-new-01"
az scvmm vm update -g $RG -n "vm-linux-new-01"   --cpu-count 8   --memory-size 16384
az scvmm vm start -g $RG -n "vm-linux-new-01"
Enabling Guest Management on SCVMM VMs

Enabling guest management installs azcmagent inside the Hyper-V VM guest OS and creates a Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines resource in Azure. This unlocks VM extensions (Azure Monitor Agent, Defender, Update Manager, Custom Script Extension) without requiring RDP or SSH access for ongoing management.

# Enable guest management on a single SCVMM VM (installs azcmagent via SCVMM Run As Account)
az scvmm vm guest-agent enable   --resource-group $RG   --vm-name "vm-win-prod-01"   --credentials-username "contoso\\svc-arc"   --credentials-password "$GUEST_CRED_PASSWORD"

# At-scale: enable guest management on all Windows VMs
az scvmm vm list -g $RG   --query "[?properties.osProfile.osType=='Windows'].name" -o tsv | while read vm; do
  echo "Enabling guest management: $vm"
  az scvmm vm guest-agent enable     --resource-group $RG     --vm-name "$vm"     --credentials-username "contoso\\svc-arc"     --credentials-password "$GUEST_CRED_PASSWORD"     --no-wait
done

# Wait for a specific VM to complete
az connectedmachine show -g $RG -n "vm-win-prod-01"   --query "{status:properties.status, agentVersion:properties.agentVersion}"

# Install Defender for Servers extension (MDE)
az connectedmachine extension create   --resource-group $RG   --machine-name "vm-win-prod-01"   --name MDE.Windows   --type MDE.Windows   --publisher Microsoft.Azure.AzureDefenderForServers   --type-handler-version 1.0

# Install Azure Monitor Agent (Windows)
az connectedmachine extension create   --resource-group $RG   --machine-name "vm-win-prod-01"   --name AzureMonitorWindowsAgent   --type AzureMonitorWindowsAgent   --publisher Microsoft.Azure.Monitor   --type-handler-version 1.22
Azure Hybrid Benefit for SCVMM VMs

Arc-enabled SCVMM VMs can be enrolled in Azure Hybrid Benefit to apply existing Windows Server Datacenter or Standard licenses to the Azure billing record. This avoids double-paying for software licenses. The benefit is applied per VM and is visible in Azure Cost Management after enrollment.

# Enable Azure Hybrid Benefit on an Arc-enabled SCVMM VM
az scvmm vm update   --resource-group $RG   --name "vm-win-prod-01"   --license-type Windows_Server

# Verify Hybrid Benefit is active
az scvmm vm show -g $RG -n "vm-win-prod-01"   --query "{licenseType:properties.osProfile.windowsConfiguration.provisionVmAgent,
            hybridBenefit:properties.licenseProfile.esuProfile.licenseType}"

# Enable ESU (Extended Security Update) for Windows Server 2012 R2 VMs via Arc
# ESUs via Arc are free for Arc-connected machines — no additional purchase required
az connectedmachine update -g $RG -n "vm-win-2012r2-01"   --set properties.licenseProfile.esuProfile.assignedLicense="Windows_Server"   --set properties.licenseProfile.esuProfile.esuEnabled=true

# List all SCVMM VMs with license type audit
az scvmm vm list -g $RG   --query "[].{name:name, licenseType:properties.storageProfile.disks[0].diskSizeGB}" -o table
SCVMM management port (from bridge VM to SCVMM)

# Validate outbound connectivity from the Arc bridge VM kubeconfig
export KUBECONFIG=./arc-scvmm-config/arc-bridge-scvmm-kubeconfig

kubectl get pods -A

# Check SCVMM provider pod logs for connection errors
kubectl logs -n azure-arc-appliance   $(kubectl get pod -n azure-arc-appliance -l app=scvmm-cloud-provider     -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') --tail=60

# Rotate SCVMM credentials stored in the bridge (no ARM re-registration)
az arcappliance update-infracredentials scvmm   --resource-group $RG   --name arc-bridge-scvmm

# Upgrade the Resource Bridge (monthly Microsoft release cadence)
az arcappliance get-upgrades -g $RG -n arc-bridge-scvmm

az arcappliance upgrade scvmm   --resource-group $RG   --name arc-bridge-scvmm   --config-file ./arc-scvmm-config/arc-bridge-scvmm-appliance.yaml
Common Mistakes with Arc for SCVMM
SCVMM admin account lacks Run As Account rights
Create dedicated Run As Account in SCVMM with admin rights on target host groups
Port 8100 blocked between bridge and SCVMM
Open port 8100 TCP from bridge VM IP to SCVMM server
Bridge deployed in wrong SCVMM host group
Bridge VM can't reach Hyper-V hosts
Redeploy bridge into a host group with connectivity to all managed Hyper-V hosts
VM template has no network adapter
az scvmm vm create fails: NIC missing
Add a virtual NIC mapped to a SCVMM VM Network in the template
Guest credentials are local (not domain) on a domain-joined VM
guest-agent enable fails with auth error
Use DOMAIN\\username format; local admin works only on workgroup machines
Run az scvmm vmmserver show to check connectionStatus; restart the scvmm-cloud-provider pod if needed
Azure Policy for Arc SCVMM VMs

Azure Policy can audit and enforce compliance on Microsoft.ScVmm/virtualMachines resources (VM-level) and on Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines resources (guest OS level, after guest management is enabled). Assign policies at the subscription or resource group scope and they apply to all connected SCVMM VMs automatically as inventory syncs.

# List built-in policy definitions that cover Arc-enabled machines (includes SCVMM VMs with guest mgmt)
az policy definition list --query   "[?contains(displayName,'Arc') && policyType=='BuiltIn'].{name:name,displayName:displayName}"   -o table | head -20

# Key policy definitions for Arc SCVMM machines:
# "Configure Arc-enabled machines running Windows Server to be associated with a Data Collection Rule"
# "Deploy Azure Monitor Agent for Windows Arc VMs"
# "Windows Arc machines should meet requirements of Azure Compute security baseline"
# "Audit Windows VMs on which Windows Defender Exploit Guard is not enabled"

# Assign policy: deploy AMA on all Windows Arc machines in scope
POLICY_DEF_ID=$(az policy definition list   --query "[?displayName=='Configure Windows Arc Machines to run Azure Monitor Agent'].name" -o tsv)

az policy assignment create   --name "ama-arc-scvmm-windows"   --display-name "Deploy AMA on Arc Windows SCVMM VMs"   --policy $POLICY_DEF_ID   --scope "/subscriptions/$SUBSCRIPTION/resourceGroups/$RG"   --assign-identity   --location eastus   --mi-user-assigned "/subscriptions/$SUBSCRIPTION/resourceGroups/$RG/providers/Microsoft.ManagedIdentity/userAssignedIdentities/arc-policy-msi"   --params '{"dcrResourceId":{"value":"'$DCR_ID'"}}'

# Trigger immediate remediation task (don't wait for 24h compliance scan)
az policy remediation create   --name "remediate-ama-arc-scvmm"   --policy-assignment "ama-arc-scvmm-windows"   --resource-group $RG

# Check remediation status
az policy remediation show   --name "remediate-ama-arc-scvmm"   --resource-group $RG   --query "{status:properties.provisioningState, succeeded:properties.deploymentSummary.successfulDeployments,             failed:properties.deploymentSummary.failedDeployments}"

# View compliance state for all Arc SCVMM VMs
az policy state list   --resource-group $RG   --filter "resourceType eq 'Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines'"   --query "[].{resource:resourceId, policy:policyDefinitionName, compliance:complianceState}"   -o table | head -30

Microsoft Defender for Servers (Plan 2) covers Arc-enabled SCVMM VMs after azcmagent is installed. Defender detects threats at the OS level through MDE (Microsoft Defender for Endpoint) integration, and provides CSPM recommendations for the SCVMM infrastructure visible in the Azure portal.

# Enable Defender for Servers Plan 2 on the subscription (covers all Arc machines)
az security pricing create   --name "VirtualMachines"   --tier "Standard"   --subscription $SUBSCRIPTION

# Verify Defender plan applies to Arc machines
az security pricing show --name "VirtualMachines"   --query "{pricingTier:pricingTier, freeTrialRemainingTime:freeTrialRemainingTime}"

# Check Defender status on a specific Arc-enabled SCVMM VM
az security assessment list   --resource-id $(az connectedmachine show -g $RG -n "vm-win-prod-01" --query id -o tsv)   --query "[?properties.status.code=='Unhealthy'].{name:displayName,severity:properties.metadata.severity}"   -o table

# KQL: Defender alerts from Arc SCVMM VMs (Log Analytics)
# SecurityAlert
# | where ProviderName == "MDATP"
# | where ResourceId contains "Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines"
# | project TimeGenerated, AlertName, Severity, CompromisedEntity, Description
# | order by Severity asc, TimeGenerated desc

# Enable MDE extension manually (if auto-provisioning is off)
az connectedmachine extension create   --resource-group $RG   --machine-name "vm-win-prod-01"   --name MDE.Windows   --type MDE.Windows   --publisher Microsoft.Azure.AzureDefenderForServers   --type-handler-version 1.0   --settings '{"azureResourceId":"'$(az connectedmachine show -g $RG -n vm-win-prod-01 --query id -o tsv)'"}'

# Verify MDE onboarding status
az connectedmachine extension show   --resource-group $RG   --machine-name "vm-win-prod-01"   --name MDE.Windows   --query "{state:properties.provisioningState,status:properties.instanceView.status}"
Update Manager: Patch Compliance for SCVMM VMs

Azure Update Manager assesses and deploys patches to Arc-enabled SCVMM VMs through the azcmagent channel. The VM must have guest management enabled. Patch schedules and maintenance configurations are the same as for Arc-enabled servers — no SCVMM-specific configuration needed.

# On-demand patch assessment for a SCVMM VM
az vm assess-patches   --resource-group $RG   --machine-name "vm-win-prod-01"   --machine-type ConnectedScVmmVirtualMachine

# Install critical + security patches now
az vm install-patches   --resource-group $RG   --machine-name "vm-win-prod-01"   --machine-type ConnectedScVmmVirtualMachine   --maximum-duration PT3H   --reboot-setting IfRequired   --classifications-to-include-windows Critical Security

# Create maintenance configuration for SCVMM fleet
az maintenance configuration create   --resource-group $RG   --name "scvmm-monthly-patch"   --maintenance-scope InGuestPatch   --location eastus   --start-date-time "2024-04-02 03:00"   --duration "04:00"   --recur-every "Month Second Tuesday"   --time-zone "Eastern Standard Time"   --reboot-setting IfRequired   --in-guest-patch-mode Platform

# Assign all Arc-enabled SCVMM Windows VMs to the maintenance config
az scvmm vm list -g $RG   --query "[?properties.osProfile.osType=='Windows'].name" -o tsv | while read vm; do
  VM_ID=$(az connectedmachine show -g $RG -n "$vm" --query id -o tsv 2>/dev/null)
  [ -z "$VM_ID" ] && continue  # skip if guest management not enabled
  echo "Assigning $vm to maintenance config"
  az maintenance assignment create     --resource-group $RG     --location eastus     --name "${vm}-patch-mc"     --maintenance-configuration-id       $(az maintenance configuration show -g $RG -n scvmm-monthly-patch --query id -o tsv)     --resource-id "$VM_ID"
done

# Compliance summary via Resource Graph
# patchassessmentresources
# | where type == "microsoft.compute/virtualmachines/patchassessmentresults"
#   or type == "microsoft.hybridcompute/machines/patchassessmentresults"
# | extend criticalMissing = properties.availablePatchCountByClassification.critical
# | extend securityMissing = properties.availablePatchCountByClassification.security
# | project name, resourceGroup, criticalMissing, securityMissing
# | where criticalMissing > 0 or securityMissing > 0
Resource Graph Queries for Arc SCVMM

// All Arc SCVMM VMs with CPU, memory, and power state
resources
| where type == "microsoft.scvmm/virtualmachines"
| extend powerState = properties.powerState
| extend cpu = toint(properties.hardwareProfile.cpuCount)
| extend memMB = toint(properties.hardwareProfile.memoryMB)
| extend template = tostring(properties.infrastructureProfile.templateName)
| project name, resourceGroup, powerState, cpu, memMB, template, location

// SCVMM VMs without guest management enabled
resources
| where type == "microsoft.scvmm/virtualmachines"
| where isnull(properties.guestAgentProfile)
| project name, resourceGroup, location

// SCVMM VMs running outdated Windows versions
resources
| where type == "microsoft.hybridcompute/machines"
| where properties.osName contains "Windows Server 2012"
    or properties.osName contains "Windows Server 2008"
| extend agentVersion = tostring(properties.agentVersion)
| extend arcStatus = tostring(properties.status)
| project name, resourceGroup, properties.osName, agentVersion, arcStatus

// Arc appliances for SCVMM (detect degraded bridges)
resources
| where type == "microsoft.resourceconnector/appliances"
| where properties.infrakind == "scvmm"
| extend status = properties.status
| extend version = properties.version
| project name, resourceGroup, status, version, location
| where status != "Running"

// Count Arc extensions deployed per SCVMM VM
resources
| where type == "microsoft.hybridcompute/machines/extensions"
| extend machineName = tostring(split(id, "/")[-3])
| summarize extensions=make_list(properties.type) by machineName, resourceGroup

Azure Arc for SCVMM extends Azure ARM governance to Hyper-V workloads managed by System Center Virtual Machine Manager. The Arc Resource Bridge VM — deployed inside the SCVMM environment — relays ARM signals to SCVMM without requiring inbound connectivity from Azure. Once connected, operators perform VM lifecycle operations from the Azure portal or CLI, create VMs from SCVMM templates, and enable guest management per VM to install AMA, Defender, and Update Manager extensions. Azure Hybrid Benefit and Extended Security Updates (ESUs) for end-of-life Windows versions are also applied through the Arc guest management layer at no additional cost.
